summary: nolimit-x ships an obfuscated phishing/spam toolkit (DKIM-replay, Microsoft device-code OAuth harvesting, contact scraping)
details: The package is distributed on npm as a self-described 'Advanced email sender' tagged 'red-team'. Its entire reachable code tree (47 files under.ad/, including the main entry x0.js) is heavily obfuscated with javascript-obfuscator (rotated string-array + RC4 decoder + self-defending wrapper + control-flow flattening), and devDependencies plus scripts.build show this obfuscation is part of the publish pipeline. Behind the obfuscation, the package implements: (1) a DKIM-replay attack in.ad/xd.js (DKIMReplayService) that drives a headless Puppeteer browser to sign into victim Gmail/Microsoft accounts and forward DKIM-signed messages through SMTP relays to deliver spoofed mail that passes authentication; (2) Microsoft device-code OAuth phishing in.ad/x12.js, abusing the well-known PowerShell first-party client_id 9e5f94bc-e8a4-4e73-b8be-63364c29d753 against https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/oauth2/v2.0/devicecode with scope `https://outlook.office.com/SMTP.Send offline_access` to mint SMTP-send tokens against phished accounts; (3) Graph/IMAP contact enumeration and inbox scraping to build target lists. A Windows-only postinstall (scripts/postinstall.js) additionally writes a `nolimit.cmd` shim into the npm global prefix pointing at.ad/x0.js, which is a quality concern but not the primary issue. The combination of full-codebase obfuscation, an explicit attack-toolkit purpose, hardcoded use of a Microsoft first-party client_id for OAuth abuse, and DKIM-replay automation puts this clearly outside the scope of legitimate npm content; installers running the CLI are pointed at infrastructure for committing fraud against third-party Microsoft/Google account holders, and the obfuscation is designed to defeat reviewer inspection.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
